images.caption: Figure 1:Top:VaSEis a KV Cache eviction method that combines stochastic sampling with value-state magnitude scoring to retain diverse and important KV pairs under a fixed KV cache budget.Bottom: By integrating both stochasticity and value awareness,VaSEoutperforms baseline methods that use either signal alone, improving average pass@1 accuracy across various reasoning tasks.

Figure 2:Left:Range\mathrm{Range}distribution of the value states. The violin plots show the presence of extreme magnitude outliers at different layers.Right: Evicting the large magnitude outliers causes accuracy to collapse to14.3%14.3\%, greatly underperforming a random eviction baseline at the same token budget, suggesting that these large-magnitude value states are crucial to model accuracy.

Figure 3:Accuracy of Qwen3-4B on GSM8K with a KV cache budget ofK=512K{=}512(∼\sim4×4\timescompression).+V16indicates reserving 16 slots in the budgetKKfor value scoring. By combining stochastic sampling with value scoring, our methodsxachieve accuracy close to the full model (88.4%88.4\%).

Figure 4:Pass@1 accuracy of Qwen3-14B under varying KV cache budgets. The full-cache model uses an average of 15.3k and 17.8k tokens on AIME26 and HMMT25, respectively. Each x-axis label reports the number of budget tokens (e.g., 2048) and its corresponding KV cache compression ratio to the full model (e.g., 7.5×\times).

Figure 5:Left: Pass@1 results of Qwen3-4B on LiveCodeBench under a 2048-token budget (∼20%{\sim}20\%of full KV). R-KV and ourVaSEmethods achieve the strongest performance.Right:Range​(𝒗)\mathrm{Range}({\bm{v}})and per-token value cache quantization errors are highly correlated under different quantization configurations, whereb2g32means 2-bit precision with a group size of 32.

Figure 6:Left & Middle: Decode throughput (↑\uparrow) of the Qwen3-14B model on a single A100-80G GPU under different KV cache budgets{\{2048, 4096, 6144}\}and total output tokens{\{16K, 32K}\}. All eviction methods run well above the original Full method (dashed line; OOM at 32K), withVaSE-DKVachieving the fastest throughput.Right: Peak GPU memory (↓\downarrow) at the 16K output tokens and 4096 budget; the 14B model weights (hatched) account for nearly 30GB of memory. All eviction methods have a much lower memory footprint than Full, withVaSE-DKVusing the least memory.

header: Appendix AVariants for Capturing Value-State Magnitude and Variety

images.caption: Figure 7:Layer-wise violin plots ofL2​(𝒗i):=‖𝒗i‖2L_{2}({\bm{v}}_{i}):=\|{\bm{v}}_{i}\|_{2}, where the value vectors𝒗i{\bm{v}}_{i}are from the full KV cache of Qwen3-4B during GSM8K generation. The distribution shows outliers that have largeL2L_{2}norm.

Figure 8:Layer-wise violin plots ofRange​(𝒗i)\mathrm{Range}({\bm{v}}_{i}), where the values𝒗i{\bm{v}}_{i}are from the full KV cache of Qwen3-4B during GSM8K generation. The distribution shows outliers that have largeRange\mathrm{Range}.

Figure 9:Examples of model outputs on GSM8K when evicting large-Range\mathrm{Range}value states. In the first example, the model starts with reasonable thinking traces but falls into a repetitive loop, never reaching the correct answer. In the second example, the model generates the correct answer once (18), but then gets stuck in an endless loop of self-reflection and fails to reach a conclusion.

Figure 10:Range​(𝒗)\mathrm{Range}({\bm{v}})over token position chunks, where each chunk consists of the value states of consecutive tokens. Boxplots illustrate the dynamic range (y-axis) of value states at specific layers. Token positions (x-axis) are bucketed to show how the range distribution evolves through the sequence. The first chunk contains sink tokens at position(0,4)(0,4). Excluding the sink tokens, the distribution ofRange​(𝒗)\mathrm{Range}({\bm{v}})does not drift as the context window grows.
Figure 11:Peak GPU memory of Qwen3-14B at 16K output length across three KV-cache budgets{2048,4096,6144}\{2048,4096,6144\}.VaSE-DKVis the most memory-efficient method at every budget.
